title: The Effect of Silica Addition on the Properties of Poly((vinylidene fluoride)-co-hexafluoropropylene)-Based Polymer Electrolytes
abstract:
866
The Effect of Silica Addition on the Properties of
Poly((vinylidene fluoride)-co-hexafluoropropylene)-
Based Polymer Electrolytes
Kwang Man Kim,* 1 Kwang Sun Ryu,1 Seong-Gu Kang,1 Soon Ho Chang,1 In Jae Chung2
1 Battery Technology Team, Electronics & Telecommunications Research Institute (ETRI),
161 Kajong, Yusong, Taejon 305-350, South Korea
2 Department of Chemical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST),
373-1 Kusong, Yusong, Taejon 305-701, South Korea
Fax: +82-42-860-6836; E-mail: kwang@etri.re.kr
Introduction
Rechargeable lithium batteries using polymer electrolytes
are now widely studied for small power sources of light,
portable electronic devices such as cellular phones, lap-
top computers, camcorders, and so on. The polymer elec-
trolytes, in either immobilized liquid electrolyte (gel
type) or hybrid polymer type, are generally favorable to
high ionic conductivity at room temperature and mechan-
ical property. Poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVdF) has been
a material of interest to use as a host component of poly-
mer gel electrolyte with high ionic conductivity and good
mechanical strength.[1] This polymer gel also proved to be
chemically stable, although ther was a slight change in
crystallinity.[2] Recently, poly(vinylidene fluoride)-co-
hexafluoropropylene),
called
P(VdF-HFP),
began
to
replace PVdF because of the high solubility and the low
crystallinity and glass transition temperature. It was con-
firmed that the P(VdF-HFP) is used as the main polymer
electrolyte in the most commonly commercialized pro-
cess of making plastic lithium-ion batteries (PLiONTM)
by Telcordia (formerly Bellcore).[3, 4] Therefore, the physi-
cal and electrochemical properties of P(VdF-HFP)-based
polymer electrolytes has been studied intensively and
extensively.[5–18]
It is also known that the addition of inorganic fillers
such as alumina (Al2O3) and silica (SiO2) to the polymer
electrolyte leads to the enhancement of physical strength
as well as the increase in the absorption level of electro-
lyte solution.[4] In the Bellcore process, however, the
fumed silica particles are just treated to play a role of
Full Paper: The effect of fumed silica (SiO2) addition on
the
physical
and
electrochemical
properties
of
poly((vinylidene fluoride)-co-hexafluoropropylene) based
polymer electrolyte was investigated by various methods
including morphology observation, thermal and crystalli-
nity evaluations, impregnation test using an electrolyte
solution, electrochemical stability analysis and impedance
measurement. About 38 wt.-% of silica content proved to
be the upper limit at which the silica could be no longer
added to obtain a self-sustained polymer electrolyte film.
As the silica content increased, a finer pore structure
developed, a greater amount of electrolyte solution was
absorbed, and thus higher ionic conductivity was shown.
The electrochemical stability was preserved up to about
4.5 V for all the polymer electrolytes prepared. The inter-
facial resistance between the polymer electrolyte and the
lithium electrode was reduced with the silica content and
also showed a diminishing growth rate with time.

 The Effect of Silica Addition on the Properties of ...
867
enhancing the mechanical strength whereas the pre-added
plasticizer such as dibutyl phthalate dominates the
absorption of electrolyte solution. That is, the dibutyl
phthalate is extracted after forming fine pore structures in
the polymer electrolyte and then replaced by electrolyte
solution. Nonetheless, recent studies of polymer electro-
lyte have a tendency of adding nano-scale inorganic fil-
lers to give a rise in electrical conductivity.[3, 19–21] For
instance, the increase in the silica content enables the
ionic conductivity to be increased by more than one order
of magnitude for P(VdF-HFP)-[3, 21] and poly(ethylene
oxide)-lithium ionic salt systems.[20]
In this paper we report the physical property changes
of P(VdF-HFP) films by varying the amount of fumed
silica particles. We also show the changes of electro-
chemical properties of the polymer films before and after
absorbing an electrolyte solution dissolved with a lithium
ionic salt. Particularly explored through this paper is the
evidence for the increase in ionic conductivity by silica
addition
and
also
discussed
is
an
applicability
to
rechargeable lithium batteries.
Experimental Part
The P(VdF-HFP) used was a commercially available fluoro-
copolymer, KynarFlexm 2801 (Elf Atochem N.A. Co.) which
contains 12 mol-% of hexafluoropropylene. The silica used
was a disilazane-treated fumed silica (Cab-O-Silm TS-530,
Cabot Co.). The polymer films were prepared by mixing the
P(VdF-HPF) powder (3–5 g) and the fumed silica (0–2 g) in
30 g of acetone solvent. Here, the silica content was adjusted
to 0.0, 4.8, 10.3, 20.0, 31.8, 35.3, and 38.0 wt.-% in the basic
polymer film consisting of P(VdF-HFP) and silica. The mix-
ture was stirred at a moderate speed for 3 h at 508C by using
a magnetic bar in a well-sealed bottle, and re-stirred at a
high speed for 24 h at room temperature. The viscous slurry
was cast on a clean glass plate using a doctor blade apparatus
(with a gap of 350 lm) in a dry atmosphere (moisture con-
tent less than 0.1 ppm), and dried in a vacuum oven at 708C
for 12 h. The self-supporting films were easily obtained with
the thickness of 27–36 lm for all compositions of silica con-
tent except for 38.0 wt.-%. Surface morphology of the dried
films was observed by a scanning electron microscope (Hita-
chi S800).
Pieces of film were then swollen for at least 24 h by
absorbing the electrolyte solution [1 m LiClO4 dissolved in
the 2:1 v/v mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl
carbonate (DMC)]. Prior to measuring the liquid weight
absorbed by the film, light-patting with a filter paper was
done several times to remove the surface liquid. The thick-
ness of the swollen film after absorbing the liquid was within
the range 31–40 lm. For the films before and after absorbing
the electrolyte solution, thermal properties were measured by
a differential scanning calorimeter using a du Pont 2100
Thermal Analyst with a 910 Cell Base and crystalline prop-
erties by a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Ka1 radia-
tion with the wavelength of k = 0.15406 nm. Chemical
instability and a crystalline phase change of the polymer
component, which might occur by the silica addition and
also by the liquid absorption, was also investigated by a
Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Bomem MB-100)
with the wavelength resolution of 4 cm–1.
Ionic conductivity of the polymer electrolytes was deter-
mined from complex impedance spectra measured using a
frequency response analyzer (Solartron HF 1225 Gain-Phase
Analyzer) in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. A
conductivity cell (2 cm62 cm) composing two stainless
steel electrodes on each side of the polymer electrolyte film
was used.
Cyclic voltammetry testing of the polymer electrolyte film
was carried out on a stainless steel electrode in a three-elec-
trode cell (2 cm62 cm) in which lithium served as both the
counter and the reference electrodes. A MacPile-II potentio-
stat/galvanostat system was used under the conditions of a
scan rate of 10 mV/s and the potential range of 0–5 V vs.
Li+/Li. The interfacial stability between lithium and the poly-
mer electrolyte was also examined by monitoring the impe-
dance of a 2 cm62 cm (Li/polymer electrolyte/Li) cell
stored at room temperature as a function of time.
Results and Discussion
In general, the mechanical property of a polymer film
may be enhanced by increasing the portion of inorganic
particles, but there is a limit of inclusion at which the
polymeric characteristics are no longer shown by the
domination of particulate features. In the present study,
the sample of 38.0 wt.-% SiO2 exhibited a poor mechani-
cal strength and a powder-like property (easily crisp
when dried), so that it failed to obtain a self-supporting
film. The other films with a lower content of SiO2 than
38 wt.-% were easily obtained and processed to measure
the properties.
Morphology and Liquid Absorption
Figure 1 shows the scanning electron microscope images
of polymer film surfaces with different contents of fumed
silica. As the silica content increases, the surface
becomes highly roughened with a smaller scale of rough-
ness unit (average distance between nearest-neighboring
pinnacles). Approximately measured roughness units are
2.8, 1.6, 1.2, 0.7, 0.5, and 0.5 lm corresponding to Fig-
ure 1a–f, respectively. For the highly filled polymer films
more than 30 wt.-% silica, a great amount of small pores
formed by the roughness unit can be also observed to be
distributed widely. The fact that small pores are formed
by the increase in silica content is supported by a recent
study of void formation by the shrinkage of polymer
material within the rigid filler matrix resulting in hetero-
geneous pore distribution.[22] Then, the surface area of the
polymer film increases with the increasing silica content
and thus the increase in the absorption of electrolyte solu-
tion is expected.

Morphology and Liquid Absorption
Figure 1 shows the scanning electron microscope images
of polymer film surfaces with different contents of fumed
silica. As the silica content increases, the surface
becomes highly roughened with a smaller scale of rough-
ness unit (average distance between nearest-neighboring
pinnacles). Approximately measured roughness units are
2.8, 1.6, 1.2, 0.7, 0.5, and 0.5 lm corresponding to Fig-
ure 1a–f, respectively. For the highly filled polymer films
more than 30 wt.-% silica, a great amount of small pores
formed by the roughness unit can be also observed to be
distributed widely. The fact that small pores are formed
by the increase in silica content is supported by a recent
study of void formation by the shrinkage of polymer
material within the rigid filler matrix resulting in hetero-
geneous pore distribution.[22] Then, the surface area of the
polymer film increases with the increasing silica content
and thus the increase in the absorption of electrolyte solu-
K. M. Kim, K. S. Ryu, S.-G. Kang, S. H. Chang, I. J. Chung
The liquid uptake (%) of polymer film was determined
by a weight increase rate as (W2 – W1)6100/W1 where
W1 and W2 were weights of polymer films before and
after absorbing an electrolyte solution, respectively. The
resulting liquid uptakes of each film are shown in Fig-
ure 2 as a function of time. Within 5 min, the polymer
films absorb more than half the saturated amounts of the
liquid electrolyte solution. After about 24 h, the liquid
As expected, the polymer film with a high silica con-
tent absorbs more liquid component, but the liquid
absorption is slightly decreased when exceeding 30 wt.-
% of silica content. Compared to the Bellcore process[4]
using a liquid plasticizer in the film formation, about
70% of the liquid uptake appeared when using more than
30 wt.-% silica which is a high value of liquid absorption
attributed to the role of silica particles. A result L100%
mass increase was reported,[16] but this was the case of re-
swollen film after the extraction of a plasticizer.
Thermal and Crystalline Properties
Figure 3 shows differential scanning calorimetric thermo-
grams of polymer and polymer electrolyte films with var-
ious silica contents. As seen in Figure 3a, the melting
behaviors on the heating run are almost the same, irrespec-
tive of the silica addition, but the heat of melting decreases
with an increase in the silica content. This means the crys-
tallinity is reduced by silica addition, which is also proved
by X-ray diffraction spectra as shown in Figure 4a. The
remaining small endotherms observed at 1608C or higher
temperatures are thought to be due to the small crystallites
which are already formed during cooling to room tempera-
ture. For instance, the small crystallites may have the ori-
ginal c-phase or the c9-phase transformed from a-phase
crystals.[23, 24] A similar pattern to these endotherms can be
seen in some results of the crystallization behavior of
The effect of silica addition is certainly reflected on the
cooling run of Figure 3a by the deviation of crystalliza-
tion temperatures. On cooling from the melt state the
silica particles may play the roles of a nucleating agent
and of accelerating the crystal growth. Thus the crystalli-
zation temperature (120.48C) of a silica-filled polymer
film is higher than that of a film with no silica (116.78C).
Scanning electron microscope surface images of
polymer films with different fumed silica contents: (a) 0.0, (b)
4.8, (c) 10.3, (d) 20.0, (e) 31.8, and (f) 35.3 wt.-% SiO2. For the
case of (d), part of an inhomogeneous surface defect is incor-
rectly focused and thus the right-side part of the image should
be considered in the discussion.
The time evolution of liquid uptake determined by
the difference in weights of polymer films before and after
absorbing the electrolyte solution.
Differential scanning calorimetric thermograms of
(a) P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2 films and (b) P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2/(1 m
LiClO4 in EC/DMC) polymer electrolytes. Temperature sweep
rates on heating and cooling are 108C and –108C per min,
respectively. The arrow for ‘increasing SiO2 content’ indicates
0.0, 4.8, 10.3, 20.0, 31.8, and 35.3 wt.-% in sequence. The
words ‘DMC’ and ‘EC’ in (b) mean the DMC-rich and EC-rich
complexes with LiClO4, respectively.
The Effect of Silica Addition on the Properties of ...
As discussed above, the liquid content of swollen poly-
mer electrolytes filled with silica varies largely with the
silica content and thus affect eventually their physical
properties. Figure 3b shows the thermograms of the poly-
mer electrolyte films in the range –90 to +908C, in which
the changes in thermal properties of the electrolyte solu-
tion component are shown. Basically, the electrolyte solu-
tion consisting of a lithium ionic salt in EC/DMC (2:1 v/
v) mixture has some thermal features[16] that EC, DMC,
and their mixture do not show any exothermic peak, but
the EC/DMC solution dissolved with a lithium salt such
as LiPF6 exhibits one or more exothermic peaks at a
lower temperature than the melting point of EC and/or
DMC. The same is true for the exotherm at –638C in Fig-
ure 3b, corresponding to the recrystallization of the
LiClO4-EC/DMC complex. However, the exotherm dis-
appears eventually when the electrolyte solution LiClO4-
EC/DMC is in an absorbed state within the polymer elec-
trolytes. Contrary to that, the polymer electrolyte includ-
ing LiPF6-EC/DMC still shows the exothermic trace.[16]
Thus it may be said that the type of lithium salt can affect
greatly the thermal properties of lithium salt-EC/DMC
and its polymer electrolyte system, and especially the
crystallization behaviors of EC and DMC components in
the polymer electrolyte. As shown in Figure 3(b), the
increase of endothermic peak areas with the silica content
may be due to increasing the content of liquid electrolyte
components. It is also seen that the endotherms corre-
sponding to EC-rich and DMC-rich LiClO4 complexes
appear to be distinguished by the increase in the silica
content and that the melting enthalpy of DMC is
increased while that of EC is decreased. These results
agree well with the fact that DMC-based substances exhi-
bit a strong endothermic event and have a tendency to
crystallize.[16] These crystallization behaviors of EC-rich
and/or DMC-rich LiClO4 complexes may inhibit fast ion
transport in the polymer electrolyte and thus lead to the
considerable reduction of ionic conductivity at low tem-
X-ray diffraction patterns for the polymer films are
shown in Figure 4 to probe the change in the crystalline
property by adding the silica and the electrolyte solution.
At first glance, the P(VdF-HFP) films show very broad
diffraction patterns, compared with PVdF homopoly-
mer,[27, 28] because of the amorphous HFP component
responsible for the reduction of crystallinity. In addition,
the increase in silica content, induces a further decrease
in crystallinity and thus broader peaks. More precisely,
the contribution of a-phase crystals on the crystallinity is
slightly undermined because the peaks around 2h = 278
and 388, characteristic of an a crystal, disappear with the
increase in silica content. Comparing Figure 4a and b, the
peak at 2h = 198, corresponding to the (110) plane of an a
crystal or the (021) of c,[28] becomes sharper due to the
local orientation of the crystal plane by the inclusion of
electrolyte solution. Thus it can be said that the compo-
nent of an electrolyte solution, such as EC, DMC, and
solvated ions from LiClO4, causes the small change of a
to c crystals. This may become evidence that the solvent
can induce a change in the crystalline phase of PVdF/sol-
The change in crystalline phase can be also confirmed
by Fourier-transform infrared spectra in Figure 5. For the
PVdF homopolymer sample, the strong vibrational bands
at 531, 766, 795, and 976 cm–1 are characteristic of the a
phase, the bands at 484 and 778 cm–1 correspond to c
phase, and the band at 840 cm–1 to b phase.[26, 27, 30] As
shown in Figure 5a, the polymer film filled with silica
particles contains a mixture of several crystalline phases
consisting of the main a crystals and small fractions of c
and b crystals. As the silica content increases, the b-phase
crystals are more developed than the c. For the polymer
electrolyte films, however, the c crystals themselves are
maintained even at the situation of high silica content but
the b crystals disappear (Figure 5b).
As seen in Figure 5, it can be said that the polymer
component shows a good chemical stability, irrespective
of adopting the fumed silica and even the electrolyte solu-
X-ray diffraction spectra of (a) P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2
films and (b) P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2/(1 m LiClO4 in EC/DMC) poly-
mer electrolytes. The arrow for ‘increasing SiO2 content’ indi-
cates 0.0, 4.8, 20.0, 31.8, and 35.3 wt.-% in sequence.
Fourier-transform infrared spectra of (a) P(VdF-
HFP)-SiO2 films and (b) P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2/(1 m LiClO4 in EC/
DMC) polymer electrolytes. The arrow for ‘increasing SiO2 con-
tent’ indicates 0.0, 4.8, 10.3, 20.0, 31.8, and 35.3 wt.-% in
K. M. Kim, K. S. Ryu, S.-G. Kang, S. H. Chang, I. J. Chung
tion. However, there seems to be a little bit of chemical
instability in that the siloxane structure (Si1O1Si anti-
symmetric stretching at L1100 cm–1 in Figure 5(a)) may
be broken to a different phase by the inclusion of electro-
lyte solution. Also the peak appeared at 727 cm–1 in Fig-
ure 5(b), corresponding to an ion-molecule association,[31]
may be evidence of the chemical instability.
Conductivities of the polymer electrolytes are calculated
from the bulk resistances determined from complex
impedance spectra. Typical impedance spectra obtained
at 238C, as shown in Figure 6a, are appeared as two types
of a slanted straight line and a slightly depressed semicir-
cular arc, depending on the silica content. The bulk resist-
ance R is determined from the intercept of the slanted line
with the real axis Z9 or from the real-axis value that the
arc reaches its minimum. Then the conductivity, r, can be
obtained from r = l/RA, where l is the thickness of the
polymer electrolyte film and A its area.
As the silica content increases, the bulk resistance is
decreased on changing the type of impedance spectrum
from the arc to the slanted line, indicating the transition
from solid-like to fluid-like electrolytes.[7] It is found that
the effect of the silica content on the spectrum type is
very similar to the temperature dependence of it. The
conductivities of the polymer electrolytes are shown in
Figure 6b over the temperature range of 3–528C. In this
figure, the ionic conductivity increases due to the increas-
ing liquid content which rises with the silica content, i.e.
degree of swelling. Also, the polymer electrolytes with
the silica content more than 30 wt.-% exhibit higher ionic
conductivities over the temperature range. The room-tem-
perature conductivities of A30 wt.-% SiO2-containing
polymer electrolytes are higher than 10–4 S/cm which is a
level applicable to the polymer electrolyte for recharge-
One may suggest that the increase of ionic conductivity
with the increase in the silica content is quite opposite,
compared to the results of thermal properties shown in
Figure 3b, because the crystallization of solution compo-
nents such as EC and/or DMC dominantly occurs so that
the ion transport is no longer fast. However, the tempera-
ture at which crystallization occurs is very low and conse-
quently the DMC-based polymer electrolyte has a tem-
perature below that at which the conductivity starts to
decrease more steeply. For instance, the temperature is in
the vicinity of –238C for the polymer electrolyte system
containing LiPF6-EC/DMC.[16] In our system containing
LiClO4-EC/DMC, the temperature reflecting the crystalli-
zation of solution components may be considered as
below 08C because the slopes of decreasing the conduc-
tivity at low temperatures become steeper with the
increase in the silica content. Indeed, the crossover of the
conductivity curves may be appeared below 08C.
Electrochemical Stability and Interfacial Resistance
The electrochemical stability of the P(VdF-HFP)-SiO2
electrolytes containing 1 m LiClO4 in EC/DMC (2:1 v/v)
was measured using cyclic voltammetry with a 262 cm2
stainless steel electrode. Figure 7(a) shows that cathodic
stability of each polymer electrolyte is moderately good,
with no electrochemical oxidation occurring until the
potential reaches 4.2 V. The polymer electrolyte with

Cyclic voltammograms of the polymer electrolyte
films containing 1 m LiClO4 in a EC/DMC (2:1 v/v) mixture on
a working electrode of stainless steel: (a) in the voltage range

The Effect of Silica Addition on the Properties of ...
35.3 wt.-% SiO2 exhibits a pre-oxidation wave starting at
about 4.0 V whereas other polymer electrolytes are pre-
oxidated at higher potentials than 4.2 V. These oxidation
voltages are lower or the electrochemical window are
slightly narrower than those of P(VdF-HFP) gelled elec-
trolytes without silica,[8] but they still have a possibility to
be used for rechargeable lithium batteries whose operat-
ing voltage is in the range of 0–4 V.
Figure 7b shows the cyclic voltammograms in the
potential range between 0 and 2.75 V, in which the anodic
and cathodic peaks are attributed by the plating and strip-
ping of lithium. Contrary to other gel-type polymer elec-
trolytes showing the current responses to stripping as
high as 0.1 mA/cm2[7] and 100 mA/cm2,[8] the present
polymer electrolytes manifest much lower stripping
responses (L0.003 mA/cm2) and consequently it may pro-
vide a promising key to the interfacial stability with
Interfacial stability of the lithium interface in the poly-
mer electrolytes is determined from the impedance spec-
tra of Li/polymer electrolyte/Li cell, which is stored at an
open-circuit condition for a long period of time. Fig-
ure 8a are typical impedance spectra as a function of
time, shown as two semicircles connected directly. Refer-
ring to the similar impedance spectra shown by Appetec-
chi et al.,[32, 33] it may be considered that the diameters of
each semicircle correspond to (i) the bulk resistance (Rbulk
= R1 – R0) of the electrolyte film and (ii) the interfacial
resistance (Rint = Rtot – R1) of the film formed on the
lithium electrode by its reaction with the electrolyte. As
shown in Figure 8b, the interfacial resistance is reduced
with the increase in the silica content and its growth rate
The results reported here show that the P(VdF-HFP)-
based polymer electrolyte can improve its physical and
electrochemical properties by the addition of fumed silica
particles. Especially the ionic conductivities of the poly-
mer electrolytes containing A30 wt.-% of silica content at
238C can be increased 2–3 orders higher than that of the
polymer electrolyte with no silica. As a result, owing to
the inclusion of silica particles, the electrolyte solution is
sufficiently absorbed into the micro-pores distributed on
the polymer electrolyte and subsequently the ionic con-
ductivity is increased though some changes in crystalline
phases occur. Associating with this fact, the silica addi-
tion also led to the reduction of the interfacial resistance
by contacting with lithium metal electrodes, which may
provide the applicability of the polymer electrolyte to
rechargeable lithium batteries.
